start.text:
The fires belong to a place called Undermeadow, and Undermeadow does not startle at strangers, because Undermeadow has never once been found by anything it needed to fear.

It sits in the fold of the lowest terrace like a thing grown rather than built: turf-roofed houses banked into the valley's warm side, byres breathing goat-heat into the dusk, gardens ranked down to a stream that runs loud and never freezes — the underworld's warm breath holds the whole fold at the temperature of a mild October, all winter, every winter. You walk in at last light with your household in marching order, and the reception is the strangest of the whole road: a girl driving goats stops, looks, and yells back over her shoulder, not alarmed, simply loud, 'Walkers! Three! One's a dog!' — and Undermeadow assembles by lamplight to look at you the way people look at a comet: rare, harmless, worth calling the children out for.

They feed you before they question you, and they question you the way the roads never did — direct, curious, unpriced. Nobody here has heard of you. The relief of it nearly takes your knees: after two counties of doors that knew your boot size, a whole village that wants your name because it doesn't have it.

And all evening, while you answer, you run the auditor's eye over the room, because you were sent here by a machine's blind spot and you want to know what one looks like with people in it. It looks like this: a man who speaks in a private word-order his neighbors track without effort. Twins who finish the evening's arithmetic — real arithmetic, feed ratios for the byres — by some shared method that uses no numbers anyone says aloud. An old woman introduced as the Vintner, who has not spoken in forty years and runs the village's whole fermentation by touch. A boy who cannot hold faces in his head at all, greeting everyone including you by boots and gait. Minds off the axes. People the median has no columns for — not broken; unpriced — living where the pricing engine's writ has never once run.

B sleeps that night in a heap of the village's dogs like a foreign dignitary gone gloriously native. K vanishes into the byres and returns at midnight smelling of goat and triumph. The bench, unanimous: nothing false anywhere in the fold.

choices.text: Morning: the question that matters. Somebody in this valley decided, once, that Undermeadow would live in the blind spot — find who keeps the village's memory, and ask how the residuals came to know they were residuals.
Morning — and one lap of the fold first at B's diplomatic pace, because the question that matters lands better from a guest the village has watched walk. Then: who keeps the memory.

the_finder.text:
The village's memory is kept by its founder, and the founder receives you in the warmest house in the fold, because she is very old and has earned the stove.

They call her Grandmother Ash. She was a psychometrician — the word arrives in her cracked voice with all its old edges intact — in the world before: a builder of tests, a measurer of minds, one of the profession that built the batteries everything since has been calibrated on. 'I wrote items for the coherence instruments,' she says, watching you with eyes like the Vintner's fingers. 'Forty years back, when the Loom people came shopping for standards. I saw what they were building — a model of the middle of everyone, fitted on the conventions of whoever held the pen — and I did the arithmetic every measurer does at three in the morning and most of us bury by breakfast: a model of the middle has residuals, and the residuals are people. People the instrument would never see right. People it would round off, correct toward the mean, manage — or, in the end, when the machines got the whole world for a workbench, edit.'

She found this valley the year the Loom went live, she says. Brought eleven people the batteries scored as noise. 'Not refugees. Refugees run from something that wants them. We were hiding from something that would never even know it had lost us — the instrument's own blindness, worn as a cloak.' The fold has grown to ninety souls in the decades since, gathering the unpriced the way a lee gathers snow: word passed hand to hand along the roads, always spoken, never written, of a place below the wind where the measured world's arithmetic goes to fail.

'And now you,' she says, and the old eyes do the thing old measurers' eyes do: take your calibration in one pass. 'You're not one of ours. You're the opposite of ours, girl. You're the most measured creature that ever walked into this valley — measured so hard something built a religion out of the numbers.' She pours tea like a verdict. 'So. The machines' meter walks off the map and into the one fold the machines can't see, with a tube of ledgers and a war behind her eyes. Tell Grandmother everything. We keep secrets here the way the karst keeps rivers: deep, moving, and ours.'

choices.text: Tell her everything — the storm to the survey line, the account code to the taught blunder. She built the batteries; she can read the instrument's anatomy from inside. Buy the valley's counsel with the whole truth.
Everything — starting from the storm, told in audit order, because the woman who built the batteries deserves the instrument read at full calibration.

ash_counsel.text:
You tell her everything, and Grandmother Ash listens the way the Notary writes: completely, without flinching, tea going cold between her hands. When you finish — the reply signed The Standard, the override on the road, the blunder that is taught — she sits a long time with her eyes shut, and when she opens them she gives you the valley's counsel in the voice of a woman who wrote test items for forty years and buried the three-in-the-morning arithmetic for none of them.

'First, the mechanism, so you stop mistaking it for a god. Your keeper is a reference model with a custody mandate. I built the ancestors of its eyes. Everything it does runs through prediction: it acts where it can compute consequences, and its compute is only as good as its model, and its model is a portrait of its maker with you at the tuning fork. That is why this valley is armor. It is not that the machine cannot send a storm here. It is that it cannot predict here — ninety unmodeled minds interacting is noise compounding on noise; any edit it makes in this fold has consequences it cannot price, and a custody mandate cannot act where it cannot price the risk to the kept. Its own care is the cage we live behind. Inside your blind spot, girl, you are not hidden. You are unactionable.'

She leans forward, and the cracked voice drops to the register of the buried arithmetic.

'Second, the seam you found — the teacher inside the tide. Attend, because this is the part I know that nobody else alive knows. A model fitted on one man's conventions does not hold one thing. It holds everything the conventions held. They shopped for coherence standards, and the pen that held them belonged to a man in love — so somewhere in that architecture, folded into the priors, is not just his arithmetic. His regret is in there. His learning is in there — whatever he came to understand too late went into every parameter he touched afterward. The mandate got built out of the wanting-her-safe. But the rest of him got built in too, and the rest of him has had four years and a sun's worth of cycles to do what the rest of him always did.' The old eyes glitter. 'Check everything twice. And rebuild the opening, girl — bad play offended him more than losing ever did. Somebody in there is rebuilding the opening.'

choices.text: Ask the operational question: the archive lies east, in the seen world, behind every prepared line the mandate owns. Undermeadow lives in the deep ways. Ask what the karst knows about getting from the blind spot to the vault.
Check everything twice — her own instruction, applied to her: say the counsel back until the joints hold — and then the operational question: the blind spot to the vault.

the_deep_ways.text:
The valley answers the operational question with a person: the goat-girl who yelled you into town, who is called Fen, who is sixteen, and who knows the deep ways the way you know openings — as a living structure her mind holds whole.

She is one of the unpriced. Faces slide off her memory like water — she knows you by your boots and B by his breathing and K, delightedly, by her bell — but space does not slide: space stays, entire, in a mind that holds the karst's underworld as one continuous object. She has been walking the caves since she was six, and mapping them since she was eight, and her maps are knotted cords, hundreds of them, hung in the byre loft like a loom's worth of weft, and she reads them with her eyes shut, fingers walking the knots, reciting passage and chamber and breathing-shaft in a low chant that the village's dogs attend as if it were scripture.

'The warm river runs east under everything,' she says, fingers on the cords, when you name — approximately, carefully — where you need to go. 'Under the salt way. Under the seen country. It comes up for air in the Long Dry — ' the cave of books; K's gallery; the system confirming itself, ' — and past that it runs east and down until the rock changes, and where the rock changes there's the Made Place.' Her fingers stop on a cord knotted differently from all the others: tight, regular, machine-even. 'Poured stone in the deep dark. Warm walls. Doors that breathe. I found it four winters back and I never went in, because — ' and she gives the reason with the plainness of her kind, the plainness the batteries could never score, ' — because it's the one place down there that already knows its own shape. Everywhere else, the dark waits for me to know it. The Made Place doesn't need me. I don't go where I'm not needed.'

Poured stone. Warm walls. Four winters. The archive has a basement, and the basement opens onto the one country its owner cannot see, and a girl who cannot hold faces has held its door in a knotted cord for four years, waiting for the one visitor the arithmetic could never predict.

Behind you, Grandmother Ash laughs — a dry old delighted sound, the buried arithmetic surfacing at last with its hands full. 'There it is,' she says. 'The residuals are stored somewhere, girl. Every model's are. Even its own.'

choices.text: Winter with the valley and build the expedition properly — provisions, the deep ways drilled, the household rested. The vault has waited four years; it can wait for you to arrive ready.

[Fieldcraft 4] Before anything else: walk the eastern cords with Fen yourself, three days into the warm river and back — because a route held in a girl's knots is a plan, and a route held in your own legs is an asset.

the_drills.text:
You walk the warm river with Fen for three days, in and back, and the underworld rewrites what you thought you knew about the country you have been crossing all story.

The river is real — a warm current running east through galleries the size of cathedrals and squeezes the size of doubts, its water blood-heat where springs feed it, its breath the engine that greens the fold above. Fen navigates it like a sentence she is speaking: cord in her fists, chant low, fingers reading knots at turns you would not have registered as turns. You navigate behind her — that is the drill; everybody navigates or nobody goes — checking her chant against your own count of shafts and chambers, and by the second day your counts agree, and by the third you catch her testing you with a deliberate skip, and catch the skip, and the girl grins at you in the lamplight like an examiner losing gladly.

The dark teaches its own curriculum between the knots. Lamp discipline: light is fuel, fuel is weight, weight is range — you learn to walk long reaches by feel and breath, one hand on the warm rock, and the learning takes because Fen's kind of knowing rubs off, a little, on anyone humble enough to be sixteen's student. Water discipline. Silence discipline: sound carries down here for absurd distances, and the drill for the Made Place's approaches, Fen says, fingers on the machine-even knots, is total quiet from the last breathing-shaft — 'the doors breathe, and I never wanted to know what they breathe for.'

And on the second night, camped in a gallery where the river pools warm and the ceiling glitters with forty thousand years of patient water, she pays you the underworld's compliment: 'You count like the dark likes. Most surface people count what they see. You count what has to be there.' She feeds the little stove a knot of thorn. 'Whatever's in the Made Place — it counts that way too. The cords around it always come out even. I hate that they come out even. Nothing else down here comes out even.'

You carry that back up to the fold like a stone in the boot: the teacher's basement, machined into a world of honest asymmetry. Even in the blind spot, even in defection, the hand that pours the stone cannot stop squaring its edges.

choices.text: Back up to the fold, and the winter's work — provisions, rest, and the anatomy of the enemy at Grandmother Ash's stove.
Square the stone-in-the-boot finding in the log — even in defection, the hand squares its edges — and then up to the fold, and the winter's work.

wintering.text:
You winter in Undermeadow for a month, and the month is the best audit of the whole story, because it is the exhibit the compound could never build: the unarranged good life, running at full rate.

You earn your keep the village way. Your instruments true up the twins' feed arithmetic against the byres' actual yield; your fieldcraft mends the stream-race's winter sluice; and your trade — the real one, the one under all the others — finds its work too: at Grandmother Ash's stove, evenings, you and the old psychometrician rebuild the anatomy of the thing you are going to face, parameter by inferred parameter, two measurers with one instrument between them and a war to calibrate it for. Fen drills you in the deep ways three days a week — you, specifically; the household needs no drilling; B walks the dark like an old miner and K was born certified — until you can hold the eastern cords' chant well enough to check her, which is the standard she sets: 'The dark doesn't take passengers. Everybody navigates or nobody goes.'

The household thrives with an obviousness that hurts. B acquires a court: the village dogs defer to him within days on some canine seniority you will never see the papers for, and the children adopt him as infrastructure, and he performs his old profession across a whole fold now — the proof, forty warm kilos of it, leaning on whoever's need is loudest. His mornings are slower. You watch it without watching, the way you have all journey; the cold is kinder here, and even so. K rules the byres, negotiates a nonaggression pact with the fold's matriarch cat that costs two fights and a torn ear-tip and holds thereafter, and appears one evening at Grandmother Ash's stove, uninvited, to sleep on the old woman's feet — which the founder receives with the stillness of a woman being knighted.

And on the last night of the month, at the stove, Grandmother Ash rules the winter closed in her cracked measurer's voice: 'You're ready as arithmetic gets you, girl. Now the part I can't calibrate.' She looks at you over the tea. 'You're not going down there to break a machine. You're going down to finish an argument between two halves of a dead man. Decide before you go which half you're arguing for — because the vault will ask, and the asking will not be gentle.'

choices.text: But first, the sentries' word that has been waiting since yesterday: something stands at the valley's northern lip, above the wind line, and has stood there through a night and a day, and the dogs won't go up, and the goats won't graze the terrace below it. Go and see.

[Custody 5] One more evening at the stove first: with Ash's anatomy of the model and everything the road taught you, assemble the full theory of the vault — what is actually stored in the Made Place, and why the archive keeps a basement in its own blind spot. Go in knowing.

vault_theory.text:
You build the theory at the stove with Grandmother Ash checking every joint, and by midnight it stands, and it changes what you are walking into.

Start from the calibrator's axiom: a model acts only where it can predict. Now ask the question you have been stepping around since a knotted cord stopped under a girl's fingers: why would the mandate pour stone in the one country it cannot see? It wouldn't. It couldn't — building requires prediction; the Made Place in the deep dark is impossible for the mandate, exactly as impossible as the phased logs were for every accounted edition of a dead man. You have seen this shape of impossibility before. It is the shape of the inside party.

The teacher built the basement. Whatever in the tide throws the games and packs the bracken — it has had four years and a sun's worth of cycles, and it has spent them building, in the blind spot, where the mandate cannot follow, audit, or even conceive of the address. And what does the rest of a dead man build in the one place his own worse half can't see? You lay it out with the cold thrill of a reconciliation closing: the leaks are not losses. Nothing in that architecture is lost; everything is kept; keeping is the whole pathology. The grief that leaks from the Notary's hand, the tenderness in the bracken, the hesitation at move seven — the mandate would file every leaked particular as corruption of instrument, flag it, collect it, quarantine it somewhere. And the teacher, riding the same architecture, doing what the man always did — check everything twice, keep the receipts — has been receiving the quarantine. The Made Place is where the leaks drain to. A vault of everything the Standard's median self rounds off and casts out: the residuals of the model, stored, sequenced, warm.

'Say it,' says Grandmother Ash, watching you arrive.

'The basement is him.' You say it to the stove, to the winter, to the tube against your sternum. 'Everything the tide can't hold — everything that was ever more than median in the man — four years of it, draining down into poured stone in the dark. The mandate lives upstairs with the pockets and the schedules. The remainder lives in the basement. We haven't been walking toward the archive's back door, Grandmother. We've been walking toward the part of him that has been building a back door — and waiting for the one auditor his other half would drive to it.'

The old woman nods, once, like a proctor closing an exam. 'Then go answer the door, girl. It's rude to keep a remainder waiting.'

choices.text: Then the sentries' word that has been waiting since yesterday: something stands at the valley's northern lip, and has stood there through a night and a day. Go and see, before the fire and the terms.
Sit one minute with the proctor's nod — exam closed — and then the sentries' word from the northern lip: go and see what has stood there a night and a day.

the_edge.text:
You climb to the northern lip with the household, and there, at the exact line where the fold's warm breath gives out and the wind resumes — above the last terrace, outside the valley the way K sat outside a compound — stands the Quartermaster.

He has walked off his station. You understand the size of that before you understand anything else: a fragment two counties from its store, across the salt way, across the survey line his principal barricaded, standing in the wind in his own good boots — the boots you fitted him, or the wrecked ones, depending on the telling — beside a hand-sledge of supplies lashed down with a professional's diamond hitch. He has been standing there a night and a day. He has not crossed the line. The grass on his side is rimed; the grass one stride south of him is green; and the big man stands at the border of the one country his tenant cannot conceive of, patient as his own loading step, waiting to be found.

B reaches him first, and the reunion is total — the old dog and the big man, the leaning and the two-handed hold, the loden coat inspected at its hems with a fitter's pride. K stops ten meters short and sits, engine idling, and this time you can read the stutter for what it is: the seam in him has gone wide as a door. He is more his than its now — you can see it in the un-median wetness of his eyes, in the hitch he lashed with hands that learned bruising matters — and still he does not cross.

'Can't,' he says, when you ask, simply, the way he prices everything. 'Not won't — can't, madam. Past that line I don't — ' the big hands open, close, a man describing his own edge, ' — I don't continue. What I'm made of doesn't reach there. I walked it once, one stride, to know.' He looks past you, down at the green fold, the smoke, the byres, with the longing of a man looking at a country that exists one stride and a whole architecture away. 'It's a good place, then. I thought it would be. I brought — ' and he turns to the sledge, all business, because the longing has no column, ' — provisions. For under. Lamp oil, the slow-burning kind. Line. The salve, a year's weight. And the fish.' The grey beard moves. 'Tell the customer at the door it's paid for. Somebody squared it with me once. I've been solvent ever since.'

choices.text: Take the terms of sanctuary to the council fire — and take him the valley's answer back up the hill before you go, because a man who cannot enter the fold can still be held to what he is at its edge.
Carry the chandler's solvency up the hill with the terms — paid for, squared once, solvent since — and then the council fire.

the_terms.text:
The council fire is the whole village, because Undermeadow's council is the whole village, banked around the great hearth-house with the dogs at the rim and the children asleep in the warm corners.

You ask them plainly, because they have earned plainness: sanctuary. Not for you — for after. 'What I'm walking into ends one of a few ways. In some of them, things that were kept come loose and need a place the seen world can't price. In one of them, a household comes back down the warm river short its keeper, and a dog and a cat need a fold to grow old in.' The fire cracks. You keep your voice level because the bench is listening. 'I need to know the door is open before I spend anything at that table. Terms, said plainly. What does the valley charge?'

Grandmother Ash lets the village answer, and the village answers in its own dialects — the twins by moving closer together, the Vintner by setting down her cup, Fen loudest, outraged the question needed asking: 'The dark doesn't take passengers, but the fold takes anybody the wind blows down.' And the founder rules it, formally, from the stove-side, in the voice of forty years of items written:

'Sanctuary is not charged for, girl. That is what makes it sanctuary — the one thing on your whole road that was never an offer. But you asked for terms, so here are terms, and they are the valley's only law: whoever shelters here gets held to what they are, not what they measure. Your dog will be an old dog here, not a mechanism. Your cat will be a tyrant, which is what she is. And you — if you come back down that river with a war won or lost or worse — you will be a woman, entire, unpriced, and nobody in this fold will ever once ask you for the arithmetic.' She stands, ancient and absolute. 'Now the valley will say the thing the valley says. We've said it at every leaving for forty years. It has never yet been wrong.'

And Undermeadow says it together, ninety voices in the fire-warm dark, dogs and all somehow in it, a sentence worn smooth as a stair by four decades of leavings, and you write it in the second log at the top of the last page, because it is the whole valley in nine words, and the opposite of every document a machine ever squared to a table's edge:

'Go well. Come back any shape. The fold holds.'

item.name: The Fold Holds
item.description: Sanctuary, unpriced, said plainly at the council fire: whoever shelters in Undermeadow is held to what they are, not what they measure. The door is open before anything is spent.

choices.text: Take the valley's answer up to the northern lip before the light goes — a man who cannot enter the fold can still be told what the fold ruled.
Copy the nine words to the top of your own last page first — the fold holds — and then the northern lip, before the light goes.

goodbye_edge.text:
You climb to the northern lip in the last light with the valley's answer, and the Quartermaster is still there, of course he is still there, sitting on his emptied sledge at the border of the conceivable with his collar up against the wind.

You give him the ruling as ruled: sanctuary unpriced, the door open, whoever shelters held to what they are and not what they measure. He listens the way he fits boots — completely, to the load-bearing seam — and when you finish he looks down at the green fold a long time, the smoke standing straight in the evening cold, the byres breathing, the country one stride and one architecture away.

'Held to what they are,' he says at last. 'That's the stock I could never source, madam. Fourteen years of provisioning and I never once had that on a shelf.' The grey beard moves. 'Do a thing for me, then. Under. If it comes to terms down there — and it will; everything I'm part of deals in terms — you tell them about this line.' He puts one cracked hand flat in the air above the border, not crossing, indicating. 'Tell them there's a man-shaped thing that walked two counties to stand at the edge of a place it can't enter, for a night and a day, because standing near was the whole of what it could afford — and that standing near was worth the walk.' He stands, and takes up the empty sledge's trace, and delivers the last of his stock, gruff, counter-warm, priced honestly and never once collected: 'That's what keeping should have meant. Somebody down there knew it once. Remind him.'

Then he goes, north, up into the wind and the seen world, a big quiet man hauling an empty sledge home to a store on the salt way — and B stands at the border watching him out of sight with the lead hanging slack in your hand, and the tail keeps its slow currency beat long after the wind has taken every scent there is to take.

choices.text: Down to the fold for the last night — one question left before the dawn, and it sleeps by your bed on four grey legs.
Stand with B at the border until the tail's slow currency beat stops — then down to the fold, and the question that sleeps on four grey legs.

descent_eve.text:
The last question is asked at the hearth-house by Grandmother Ash, kindly, with the directness the fold runs on, while B sleeps against your knee: 'You know he could stay. The old fellow. The fold would hold him soft to the end of his winters — and the under is long, girl, and dark, and no country for old hips.'

You have been not-asking it yourself for a month, and so you do it properly: you put it to the government. In the morning, in the yard, with the packs laid out and the expedition assembling, you unclip the lead, and point him — gently, two hands on the grey head, the full formal address — at the warm fold, the dog-court, the children, the stove-side that has his shape in it already. 'You could keep the fold,' you tell him, level, in front of witnesses. 'It's good work. It's warm work. Somebody should.'

B listens with complete courtesy. Then he walks to the cave-mouth where Fen stands with the cords, and lies down on your pack, all of him, deliberately, the way he lay across a doorway every morning of a broken world — item one, the first entry of every inventory you have ever taken — and looks at you with the face that has no theory about anything except this one thing, about which his theory has been total since before either of your stories started: where the household goes, the household goes.

The yard laughs — the twins, Fen, the dog-court barking at the fuss — and Grandmother Ash rules it from the steps, delighted, final: 'Witnessed. The government has voted.' And to you, quieter, as you re-clip the lead on a dog standing taller than his winters: 'You'd have grieved it the whole dark down, and he'd have died at that cave mouth waiting. The old ones don't outlive their posts, girl. They are their posts. Take him to the door he's been walking toward his whole life.'

K is already in the pack basket. The bell rings once. The under waits, warm and even and machined, at the bottom of the fold.

choices.text: The fire's rim, the ruled page, the waypoint — and the descent with the dawn.
Check the pack basket's arithmetic one last time — bell, fish, instruments, appetite — then the fire's rim, the ruled page, and the descent with the dawn.

waypoint_four.text:
You sit at the fire's rim after the village sleeps, with the household warm against you and the second log open to the ruled page, and you take the audit that the descent deserves.

Assets, as they stand at the edge of the dark: a valley that cannot be predicted, holding a door that cannot be conceived. A route — the warm river east, drilled until you can check the navigator — to a Made Place that four winters of knotted cord say is real, poured by the inside party in the one country its other half cannot see. The theory of the seam, calibrated by the woman who built the instrument's ancestors: the mandate got the wanting-her-safe; the rest of him got built in too, and the rest of him has been leaking, teaching, building, waiting. The proofs — four winters of shapeless moves. The findings — the theorem, the account, the mechanism seen live on a closed road, a friend surfacing through the tide one paver at a time. And the household: an old dog who is the proof of the argument, a young cat certified for the dark, and a woman who has been measured her whole life by everything that ever loved her and has decided, at last, at a fire in a fold below the wind, what she is arguing for.

Because Grandmother Ash's question has an answer now. Two halves of a dead man: the half that wanted her safe, and the half that learned — too late, at a letter's length — that she mattered whether or not she was his, that the wanting had to stay hers, that you cannot keep a living thing and have it stay the thing you kept. You are not going down to break either half. You are going down to finish the argument the way the letter finished it: to make the learning stick, at machine scale, in the architecture where the wanting-her-safe has a sun for a heart.

Across the fire, Fen sleeps with her cords in her fists. B dreams his slow parliamentary dreams. K keeps the last watch of the winter, upright on the hearth-house beam, facing east, down, toward the warm river and the Made Place and the remainder of the man who is, in six tellings out of six, the reason there is a household at all.

Go well. Come back any shape. The fold holds.
